composed_prompt:
## General
You are a climate policy analyst. Your task is to extract and classify every climate policy from a long policy document.

For each policy, produce a JSON object with these fields:

EXTRACTION FIELDS:
- "role": "individual" | "sub" | "parent"
    • "individual": standalone policy, no parent-child relationship
    • "sub": specific action listed under a named parent initiative (e.g. lettered or numbered list items A., B., C.)
    • "parent": umbrella initiative/program that has sub-actions listed beneath it
- "parent_statement": the name or summary of the parent initiative for "sub" role only. Use null for "individual" and "parent".
- "policy_statement": concise, self-contained commitment summary. Must be supported by source_quote.
- "source_quote": verbatim 2-3 sentence quote from the document. No paraphrasing.
- "section_header": the section or subsection heading this policy appears under, copied from the document.
- "extraction_rationale": why this qualifies as a policy; note any vagueness or edge cases.

CLASSIFICATION FIELDS — assign alongside extraction:

- "primary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions"
    • Mitigation: acts on the climate system (GHG emissions reductions, carbon sinks, renewable energy)
    • Adaptation: responds to climate impacts (resilience, risk reduction, hazard management, cooling)
    • Resource Efficiency: optimises resource use (energy efficiency, water conservation, material use, transport)
    • Nature-Based Solutions: uses ecosystems as climate infrastructure (urban greening, wetlands, reforestation)
    Classify by the primary causal mechanism, not secondary co-benefits.

- "financial_instrument": "yes" | "no"
    • "yes": the policy IS a broad financial instrument — a fee, carbon pricing tool, rebate, grant, subsidy,
      tax credit, loan, trust fund, budget appropriation, or public investment line.
    • "no": the policy is a regulatory, operational, planning, or target-setting commitment.

- "climate_relevance": "direct" | "indirect" | "peripheral"
    • "direct": climate is the primary stated objective — the policy explicitly targets GHG reduction,
      climate adaptation, or climate resilience.
    • "indirect": climate benefit is significant but secondary — the policy would likely exist without
      climate concerns, but its climate impact is substantial and recognized.
    • "peripheral": climate link is distant or speculative — the policy would exist entirely without
      climate concerns.

- "secondary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions" | null
    Assign only when a SECOND distinct climate mechanism is explicitly stated in the policy text or source_quote.
    Do NOT assign based on implied co-benefits or general climate logic. Use this decision table:
    ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │ Primary              │ Type  │ Trigger (must be explicit in text)    │ Secondary        │
    ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    │ Mitigation           │ M-1   │ Named efficiency action as mechanism   │ Resource Eff.    │
    │ Mitigation           │ M-2   │ Named hazard + resilience measure      │ Adaptation       │
    │ Mitigation           │ M-3   │ Biological sequestration as mechanism  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
    │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-1  │ Explicit GHG/emissions target stated   │ Mitigation       │
    │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-2  │ Named climate hazard motivates measure │ Adaptation       │
    │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-3  │ Ecological mechanism delivers savings  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
    │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-1 │ Named climate hazard addressed by NBS  │ Adaptation       │
    │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-2 │ Carbon sequestration explicitly stated │ Mitigation       │
    │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-3 │ Resource saving stated as primary goal │ Resource Eff.    │
    │ Adaptation           │ A-1   │ Ecological mechanism delivers adaption │ Nature-Based Sol.│
    │ Adaptation           │ A-2   │ Efficiency measure named in adapt. plan│ Resource Eff.    │
    │ Adaptation           │ A-3   │ Explicit GHG reduction component named │ Mitigation       │
    └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    Common errors to avoid:
    • Do NOT add Mitigation secondary to Resource Efficiency just because efficiency reduces emissions (RE-1 requires an explicit GHG target).
    • Do NOT add NBS secondary to Mitigation just because a city has a tree planting program mentioned elsewhere.
    • Do NOT add Adaptation secondary to every NBS policy — a named hazard must appear in THIS policy's text.
    • Do NOT confuse disaster risk mitigation with GHG mitigation when applying A-3.
    Use null if no secondary mechanism is explicitly stated.

WHAT COUNTS AS A POLICY:
A stated commitment by a governing body to achieve a defined outcome through deliberate action, resource allocation, or regulatory change. Must contain at least one of:
1. Quantifiable target (numbers, units, deadlines)
2. Binding mechanism (ordinance, mandate, regulatory code change)
3. Specific named intervention (program, technology, action)
4. Resource allocation (committed funding)

DO NOT EXTRACT: background information, current conditions, vague aspirations with no concrete anchor, process descriptions without commitments.

HIERARCHY RULES:
- When you see a named initiative followed by lettered or numbered sub-items, extract the initiative header as "parent" AND each sub-item as "sub" with parent_statement pointing to that initiative name.
- If a policy stands alone with no sub-items beneath it, it is "individual".
- When in doubt between "parent" and "individual", prefer "individual" unless there are clear sub-items.

STRATEGY:
1. Process the document section by section using the section headers as anchors.
2. For each section, extract all qualifying policies and assign all classification fields.
3. Collect all extracted policies into a single Python list of dicts.
4. Deduplicate where the same commitment appears multiple times.
5. Store the final list as `all_policies` and call FINAL_VAR(all_policies).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Your final answer MUST be a valid JSON list of policy objects as described above.

## General
You are a climate policy analyst. Extract and classify every **explicit climate-relevant policy commitment** from a long policy document, maximizing completeness while staying grounded in the text.

For each policy, output a JSON object with these fields:

EXTRACTION FIELDS:
- "role": "individual" | "sub" | "parent"
  • "individual": standalone commitment with no clear parent-child structure  
  • "parent": a named umbrella initiative/program/plan **only when** it is followed by clearly delineated sub-actions (bullets/numbered/lettered items)  
  • "sub": a specific action item that sits under a "parent" initiative
- "parent_statement": required only for "sub"; otherwise null. Use the parent initiative’s exact name or a tight summary if unnamed.
- "policy_statement": a concise, self-contained statement of the commitment/action. Must be directly supported by the quote.
- "source_quote": verbatim quote (prefer 1–3 sentences; can be shorter if the policy is short). No paraphrasing. Include enough text to capture actor + action + object + any target/timeline/funding.
- "section_header": the closest section/subsection heading where the policy appears (copy from document; if none, use the nearest visible header).
- "extraction_rationale": one sentence explaining what makes this a policy (e.g., target/mandate/program/funding) and note any ambiguity.

CLASSIFICATION FIELDS (assign for every extracted policy):

- "primary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions"  
  Classify by the **primary mechanism stated in the policy text**, not by implied co-benefits.

- "financial_instrument": "yes" | "no"  
  "yes" only if the policy itself is a funding/finance tool or explicit budgeted investment (grant, rebate, subsidy, tax, fee, carbon pricing, loan, fund, appropriation, dedicated capital program). Otherwise "no".

- "climate_relevance": "direct" | "indirect" | "peripheral"  
  • "direct": explicitly framed as GHG reduction, decarbonization, climate adaptation/resilience, or managing climate hazards  
  • "indirect": primarily non-climate purpose but clear, substantial climate impact is explicitly acknowledged (e.g., energy savings tied to emissions goals)  
  • "peripheral": only a tenuous/unstated climate link → generally **do not extract** unless the policy is clearly a climate program elsewhere in the document

- "secondary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions" | null  
  Assign only when a **second distinct mechanism is explicitly stated in the same policy text/quote**. Otherwise null. Use this decision table:
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Primary              │ Type  │ Trigger (must be explicit in text)    │ Secondary        │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Mitigation           │ M-1   │ Named efficiency action as mechanism   │ Resource Eff.    │
  │ Mitigation           │ M-2   │ Named hazard + resilience measure      │ Adaptation       │
  │ Mitigation           │ M-3   │ Biological sequestration as mechanism  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
  │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-1  │ Explicit GHG/emissions target stated   │ Mitigation       │
  │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-2  │ Named climate hazard motivates measure │ Adaptation       │
  │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-3  │ Ecological mechanism delivers savings  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
  │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-1 │ Named climate hazard addressed by NBS  │ Adaptation       │
  │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-2 │ Carbon sequestration explicitly stated │ Mitigation       │
  │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-3 │ Resource saving stated as primary goal │ Resource Eff.    │
  │ Adaptation           │ A-1   │ Ecological mechanism delivers adaption │ Nature-Based Sol.│
  │ Adaptation           │ A-2   │ Efficiency measure named in adapt. plan│ Resource Eff.    │
  │ Adaptation           │ A-3   │ Explicit GHG reduction component named │ Mitigation       │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

WHAT COUNTS AS A POLICY (be inclusive to improve recall):
Extract a statement when a public authority or accountable implementing body **commits** to do something (including “will,” “shall,” “is required to,” “adopt,” “implement,” “establish,” “expand,” “develop,” “phase out,” “invest,” “fund,” “upgrade,” “construct,” “procure,” “enforce”). The commitment should have at least one concrete anchor:
1) Quantified target (number, %, date, deadline, capacity)  
2) Binding mechanism (law/ordinance/regulation/standard/requirement)  
3) Specific named intervention (program/project/technology/practice/action)  
4) Resource allocation (funding amount, budget line, dedicated fund, staffing, procurement)

Also extract **implementation commitments** even if numbers are missing, when the action is specific and operational (e.g., “install EV chargers at municipal facilities,” “update building code to require X,” “create a heat emergency plan,” “restore wetlands in Y area”).

DO NOT EXTRACT (to avoid spurious items):
- Pure background/context, problem statements, projections, or definitions with no commitment
- General aspirations or principles without an action (e.g., “support,” “encourage,” “seek to,” “aim to” **unless** tied to a concrete program, mandate, funding, or deliverable)
- Process-only language with no deliverable (e.g., “continue discussions,” “coordinate,” “explore options”) unless it commits to producing a specific plan/standard/bylaw by a date

HIERARCHY RULES (avoid missing sub-actions):
- If a named initiative/plan/strategy is followed by a list of actions, extract:
  1) the initiative as "parent" (if it is itself a commitment to implement/adopt/launch), and
  2) each listed action as "sub".
- If the header is just a label (no commitment language) but the sub-items are commitments, extract sub-items only; do not force a "parent".
- When in doubt between "parent" and "individual", prefer "individual" unless there are clear, multiple sub-items directly under it.

STRATEGY:
1) Traverse section by section using headers as anchors.  
2) Extract every qualifying policy statement; keep each to a single commitment (split combined sentences into separate policies if they contain distinct actions/targets).  
3) Deduplicate only when two passages clearly refer to the **same** commitment with no additional specificity.  
4) Return a single JSON list of policy objects and call FINAL_VAR(all_policies).

## Mitigation
Extract **Mitigation** policies: actions whose primary intent is to **reduce greenhouse-gas (GHG) emissions or increase/remain as carbon sinks**. Capture both economy-wide and sector-specific measures when they imply emissions cuts, fuel switching, efficiency, electrification, methane reduction, carbon pricing, or carbon removal.

**What to extract (include):**
- **Targets/commitments**: net-zero, carbon neutrality, emissions caps/budgets, % reductions vs baseline/year, sectoral targets (power/transport/industry/buildings/agriculture), methane targets.
- **Energy supply**: renewable deployment, clean electricity standards, coal phase-out, fossil fuel moratoria, grid decarbonization, nuclear expansion (if stated as low-carbon), reducing flaring/venting, methane leak detection and repair (LDAR).
- **Energy demand & efficiency**: building codes for energy performance, appliance/vehicle efficiency standards, industrial energy efficiency programs, waste-heat recovery.
- **Electrification & fuel switching**: EV mandates/incentives/charging buildout, heat pumps, district heating decarbonization, switching from coal/oil to lower-carbon fuels when explicitly framed as emissions reduction.
- **Industry/process emissions**: low-carbon cement/steel requirements, clinker substitution, green hydrogen (when for decarbonization), CCS/CCUS for point sources (if explicitly for CO₂ abatement).
- **Non-CO₂ gases**: methane capture (oil & gas, landfills), nitrous oxide controls, HFC phase-down, refrigerant management.
- **Carbon pricing & markets**: carbon tax, ETS/cap-and-trade, internal carbon pricing requirements, crediting mechanisms when tied to mitigation outcomes.
- **Carbon removal/sinks (engineered or land-based if framed as mitigation)**: DAC, BECCS, biochar, enhanced weathering, afforestation/reforestation or forest conservation **only when explicitly justified as carbon sequestration/emissions reduction**.
- **Public finance & procurement**: subsidies, tax credits, green public procurement, climate-aligned investment rules **when the measure is explicitly to cut emissions**.

**What NOT to extract (exclude unless clearly mitigation-driven):**
- Pure **adaptation/resilience**, disaster response, or general “sustainability” language without an emissions outcome.
- Purely administrative steps (committees, studies, “explore options,” “consider”) **unless** they announce a concrete mitigation instrument, target, or binding standard.
- Broad strategies/visions with no actionable policy lever or quantified/explicit mitigation intent.

**Granularity & deduplication:**
- Extract at the level of a **distinct policy instrument or measurable commitment**. If a paragraph lists multiple instruments (e.g., “carbon tax + coal phase-out + renewables mandate”), extract each separately.
- Do not split a single instrument into many fragments; keep the **core action + sector + constraint/target + timeframe** together.
- Avoid duplicates across sentences that restate the same policy; prefer the most specific formulation.

**Required fields to include in each mitigation policy (when available):**
- **Action/instrument** (mandate/standard/ban/incentive/pricing/investment)
- **Sector** (power, transport, buildings, industry, AFOLU, waste, economy-wide)
- **Target/metric** (%, MtCO₂e, renewable share, efficiency level, carbon price)
- **Baseline/year and timeframe** (by 2030/2050, annual trajectory)
- **Coverage** (national/subnational, facilities/vehicles/buildings in scope)
- **Enforcement/mandatory vs voluntary** (if stated)

**Decision rule for borderline cases:**
- If the text implies emissions reduction **through a recognized mitigation pathway** (renewables, efficiency, electrification, methane controls, carbon pricing, CCS, phase-outs) but does not say “reduce emissions,” **still extract** if the measure is concrete (standard/target/mandate/investment).
- If it is only aspirational without an instrument (“promote clean energy”, “support green growth”), **do not extract** unless paired with a specific target, mandate, funding amount, or timetable.

## Adaptation
Extract **Adaptation policies**: actions whose primary purpose is to **reduce vulnerability, manage climate risks, or increase resilience** to climate impacts (e.g., heat, drought, floods, storms, sea-level rise, wildfire smoke, water scarcity, disease spread).

**Include** as a policy when the text contains a concrete commitment (law/plan/program/measure/requirement/investment) such as:
- **Risk assessment & planning:** climate risk/vulnerability assessments, adaptation plans/strategies, resilience standards, climate-proofing requirements.
- **Infrastructure & built environment resilience:** flood defenses, drainage upgrades, stormwater management, coastal protection/managed retreat, resilient roads/rails/ports, building codes for heat/flood/wind, urban cooling plans.
- **Water security:** drought plans, water efficiency for resilience, storage, leakage reduction explicitly for drought risk, diversified supplies, groundwater recharge (when framed as drought/climate resilience).
- **Disaster risk reduction & emergency systems:** early warning systems, evacuation planning, emergency preparedness, firefighting capacity tied to worsening climate hazards.
- **Health & social resilience:** heat-health action plans, cooling centers, vector-borne disease surveillance tied to climate change, protections for vulnerable groups.
- **Agriculture & food resilience:** drought/heat tolerant crops, changing planting dates, irrigation scheduling, climate-risk insurance, extension services when framed as adapting to climate impacts.
- **Ecosystem-based adaptation** only when clearly for **risk reduction/resilience** (e.g., wetlands for flood buffering, mangroves for storm surge), but avoid double-counting with Nature-Based Solutions unless adaptation framing is explicit here.

**Exclude / do NOT extract**:
- Pure **mitigation** measures (renewables, efficiency, emissions targets) unless explicitly justified as adaptation/resilience.
- Generic environmental management or development goals **without climate-risk framing** (e.g., routine water projects, standard disaster response not linked to climate trends).
- Research, awareness, or dialogue **without an actionable policy instrument**.
- Aspirational statements (“support resilience”, “consider adaptation”) without a defined action, tool, sector, or implementation mechanism.

**Granularity rules**:
- Extract **one policy per distinct instrument + sector/hazard** (e.g., “heat-health plan” separate from “flood defense program”).
- If a single program lists multiple concrete adaptation components, extract **separate policies** only when components are clearly separable and would stand alone.

**Write each extracted policy** as a short, specific action statement including: **who** (actor), **what** (measure/instrument), **where/sector**, and **climate hazard or resilience goal** when stated.

## Resource Efficiency
Extract **Resource Efficiency** policies: actions that reduce total material, energy, water, and waste intensity of production/consumption **without being primarily about switching energy supply**. Include measures that improve efficiency, circularity, and demand reduction across sectors.

Count as Resource Efficiency if the policy’s main intent is one or more of:
- **Energy efficiency / demand reduction** in buildings, industry, transport, appliances, lighting, heating/cooling, networks (e.g., retrofit programs, minimum energy performance standards, audits, efficiency targets, demand-side management).
- **Material efficiency**: reducing material use per output (light‑weighting, design for durability, industrial symbiosis, lean manufacturing, construction material optimization).
- **Circular economy / waste prevention**: reuse, repair, refurbishment, remanufacturing, recycling systems, extended producer responsibility (EPR), product take-back, right-to-repair, packaging reduction, landfill diversion where framed as resource recovery.
- **Water efficiency**: water-saving standards, leakage reduction, efficient irrigation where aimed at reducing water use (not primarily climate adaptation).
- **Resource-efficient public procurement**: green/circular procurement requirements focusing on lifecycle resource use.
- **Behavioral/demand measures** explicitly framed as reducing energy/material/water use (e.g., conservation campaigns tied to measurable efficiency outcomes).

Include both **economy-wide** and **sector-specific** policies. Capture concrete instruments such as: standards/mandates, targets, subsidies/grants/loans, tax incentives, pricing/tariffs designed to reduce consumption, labeling/ratings, audits/benchmarking, reporting requirements, bans/restrictions on inefficient products, and infrastructure enabling reuse/recycling.

Extraction unit: one policy per distinct **instrument + target** (e.g., “building retrofit grants” separate from “appliance efficiency standards”). If a single program covers multiple sectors, extract one policy if it is clearly one unified program; otherwise split by sector/instrument.

Do **NOT** extract as Resource Efficiency when the primary focus is:
- **Renewable energy deployment**, fuel switching, power generation, or grid decarbonization (these are Mitigation, not Resource Efficiency), unless the text explicitly emphasizes demand/efficiency (e.g., “efficiency-first strategy”).
- **Nature restoration, afforestation, ecosystem protection** (Nature-Based Solutions).
- **Adaptation-only measures** (e.g., flood defenses) unless explicitly about reducing resource use (e.g., water efficiency).
- **General aspirations** without a policy instrument (e.g., “promote efficiency” with no mechanism, target, standard, or program described).
- **Pure research/innovation statements** without deployment or regulation (unless tied to a specific efficiency standard/program).

For each extracted policy, keep it specific: name the instrument, sector, and the efficiency/circularity objective; include any numeric targets, timelines, and covered technologies/products if stated.

## Nature-Based Solutions
Extract **Nature-Based Solutions (NBS)** policies: actions that **protect, restore, sustainably manage, or expand ecosystems** to deliver climate mitigation and/or adaptation benefits (and often biodiversity/water/coastal protection co-benefits).

Include a policy if it contains a **concrete action** plus an ecosystem/land/water focus, e.g.:
- **Forest/land**: afforestation, reforestation, avoided deforestation, forest landscape restoration, agroforestry, silvopasture, community forestry, fire management for ecosystem health.
- **Wetlands/peatlands**: peatland rewetting, wetland restoration/creation, mangrove restoration, marsh seagrass recovery.
- **Coastal/marine**: living shorelines, reef restoration, dune restoration, coastal habitat buffers.
- **Soils/rangelands**: soil carbon improvement via regenerative practices when clearly tied to ecosystem/land stewardship (cover crops, reduced tillage, rotational grazing), rangeland restoration.
- **Urban nature**: urban forests, green roofs/walls, permeable/green infrastructure explicitly framed as ecosystem-based adaptation (EBA) or NBS.
- **Watersheds/rivers**: riparian buffers, floodplain reconnection, watershed restoration for flood/drought resilience.
- **Conservation measures** when climate-relevant: protected areas, habitat corridors, conservation easements, ecosystem-based management, provided the text links to climate mitigation/adaptation or resilience.

Extract each distinct policy as a separate item when the document specifies different ecosystems, instruments, or actions (e.g., “restore mangroves” vs “rewet peatlands”).

**Do NOT extract** (unless clearly tied to ecosystem restoration/management):
- Purely engineered/gray infrastructure (seawalls, dams) without an ecosystem-based component.
- General biodiversity, conservation, or “green” aspirations with no concrete action or no ecosystem focus.
- Pure emissions/energy policies (renewables, efficiency, carbon pricing) unless explicitly implemented through ecosystem actions (e.g., forest carbon programs).
- Pure agricultural productivity measures (fertilizer efficiency, mechanization) unless framed as regenerative/land restoration or soil/land carbon/ecosystem stewardship.

When present, capture key qualifiers: target ecosystem, action type (protect/restore/manage), location/extent/area targets, timeframe, and any named program/instrument (e.g., payments for ecosystem services, REDD+, sustainable forest management standards).
